refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both directions of communication, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote scenarios. Log p95 latency metrics across multiple operational sessions. Validate that latency remains within bounds during peak system load and network stress conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire sensor data (position, orientation, environmental) from the vehicle and transmit to the remote operator interface with latency ≤ 75 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall receive operator commands from the remote interface and transmit to the vehicle control subsystem with latency ≤ 75 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall implement network link monitoring and adaptive transmission strategies to maintain latency compliance under variable network conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall provide latency visibility and alerts to the operator and safety manager when latency approaches or exceeds safe thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a dedicated low-latency teleoperation protocol with hardware-accelerated packet processing, prioritized network queuing, and edge-based sensor aggregation to minimize end-to-end delay.
DP-B: Deploy a cloud-based teleoperation relay with optimized routing, predictive buffering, and operator-side latency compensation (dead-reckoning, local control loops) to mask network variability.

DP-C: Establish a hybrid architecture combining local vehicle autonomy (for immediate safety-critical responses) with remote operator oversight, reducing latency sensitivity through intelligent task delegation.
DP-D: Utilize a multi-path network strategy with redundant communication channels (cellular, satellite, mesh) and automatic failover to maintain latency compliance across diverse network conditions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement a dedicated low-latency teleoperation protocol with hardware-accelerated packet processing, prioritized network queuing, and edge-based sensor aggregation to minimize end-to-end delay.

potential_sources: DP-A couples tightly to network infrastructure design (must support prioritized queuing and edge processing capabilities).
DP-A couples to vehicle sensor architecture (requires deterministic sensor sampling and aggregation).
DP-A couples to operator interface design (must support low-latency rendering and input capture).
DP-A has loose coupling to safety services (safety monitoring is orthogonal to latency optimization).
coupling_check.notes: DP-A is selected because it directly addresses the root cause of latency (protocol overhead, processing delays) rather than masking symptoms. It is implementable within the NGHE architecture and aligns with high safety criticality by maintaining deterministic, auditable communication paths. Couplings are manageable through clear interface contracts.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports hardware-accelerated packet processing and prioritized queuing (QoS capabilities).
Assumption: Vehicle sensor subsystem can provide deterministic, synchronized data streams at required sampling rates.
Assumption: Operator interface hardware (display, input devices) supports sub-50 ms input-to-output latency.
Risk: If network does not support QoS, latency targets may not be achievable; mitigation requires DP-D (multi-path redundancy).
Risk: Sensor jitter or asynchronous data arrival could violate latency bounds; mitigation requires sensor synchronization and buffering strategy.
Risk: Operator interface latency could dominate end-to-end budget; mitigation requires careful hardware selection and validation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify: (1) maximum acceptable latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition, network transit, and operator interface rendering; (2) protocol specification (e.g., custom UDP-based vs. ROS 2 DDS with QoS tuning); (3) edge processing location (vehicle-side, network gateway, or operator-side); (4) fallback strategy if latency compliance cannot be maintained.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency optimization logic, including protocol implementation, packet prioritization, sensor data aggregation, operator command routing, and latency monitoring. It interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor data and actuator commands), Safety Services (for latency-related safety alerts), and external Network Link (for communication transport).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives sensor data (position, orientation, environmental state) from Vehicle subsystem. Must support deterministic, low-latency data streaming with timestamps for latency measurement.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Transmits operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) to Vehicle subsystem. Must guarantee command delivery and latency compliance for safety-critical operations.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Sends latency status and alerts to Safety Services when latency approaches or exceeds safe thresholds. Enables safety manager to intervene or trigger failsafe behaviors.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with external network infrastructure. Teleoperation module must manage packet prioritization, QoS negotiation, and adaptive transmission strategies over this link.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning DP-A to NGHE Solution would diffuse latency responsibility across multiple sub-modules and obscure ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns sensor acquisition and actuator control. However, latency optimization (protocol, network queuing, edge processing) extends beyond vehicle boundaries. Assigning DP-A to Vehicle would couple vehicle design to network architecture, violating separation of concerns. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation latency and control. DP-A (low-latency protocol, packet prioritization, sensor aggregation) is entirely within Teleoperation's scope. Clear ownership, minimal diffusion. Recommended assignment.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alerts. Latency optimization logic is not a safety service; it is a teleoperation capability. Assigning DP-A to Safety Services would conflate safety monitoring with latency control. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP-A because it encapsulates the protocol, network optimization, and sensor aggregation logic required to achieve low-latency remote operation. Teleoperation has clear interfaces to Vehicle (Telemetry, Tasking), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link, enabling it to implement the full DP-A strategy without diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module does not have direct control over network QoS settings, DP-A may be constrained by network infrastructure limitations. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contract with Network Link specifying QoS requirements.

Risk: If Vehicle sensor subsystem does not provide synchronized, deterministic data streams, Teleoperation cannot achieve latency targets. Mitigation: Define Telemetry interface with strict timing guarantees (e.g., max jitter, sampling rate).

Risk: Operator interface latency (display rendering, input capture) may dominate end-to-end budget, making Teleoperation module unable to meet FR. Mitigation: Allocate latency budget across all components; validate operator interface separately.

Risk: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it attempts to process all sensor data and commands. Mitigation: Implement edge processing (on vehicle or network gateway) to distribute computational load.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module scope to explicitly define: (1) latency budget allocation (e.g., 40 ms sensor-to-network, 30 ms network transit, 40 ms network-to-operator); (2) protocol specification and implementation language; (3) edge processing strategy (where aggregation and prioritization occur); (4) interfaces to Vehicle and Network Link with timing guarantees; (5) monitoring and alerting mechanisms for latency compliance.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (low-latency protocol with hardware acceleration, prioritized queuing, edge aggregation) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms p95). The DP is specific, implementable, and traceable to the FR. No independence violations detected; DP-A is a coherent solution concept.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk: If Teleoperation module does not own sensor aggregation logic, that logic may be duplicated in Vehicle or Safety Services modules. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership boundary in Teleoperation; define Telemetry interface as the contract.

Diffusion Risk: If network QoS configuration is owned by a separate Network Infrastructure module, Teleoperation may lack control over prioritization. Mitigation: Define Network Link interface with explicit QoS negotiation capabilities.

Diffusion Risk: Operator interface latency (display, input) may be owned by a separate UI module. Teleoperation cannot control this; latency budget must account for it. Mitigation: Allocate latency budget across all components; validate UI separately.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-A. The module owns protocol logic, packet prioritization, and sensor aggregation—the core elements of DP-A. External dependencies (Vehicle sensors, Network Link, operator interface) are managed via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Containment is appropriate and diffusion is minimal.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-008 is verifiable through end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with mock Vehicle and Network Link) and integrated with real hardware. Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) are allocated to Teleoperation with clear ownership. Interfaces to Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link are explicit. No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition (Vehicle), network transit (Network Link), and operator interface rendering (UI module). Ensure budget is realistic and achievable.
Define Telemetry interface with explicit timing guarantees (sampling rate, max jitter, timestamp accuracy) to enable Teleoperation to meet latency targets.
Specify protocol details (UDP vs. TCP, custom vs. standard, serialization format) to enable implementation planning and risk assessment.
Establish fallback strategy if latency compliance cannot be maintained (e.g., switch to autonomous mode, reduce operator command frequency, trigger safety alert).
Plan for latency monitoring and observability (logging, metrics, dashboards) to support verification and operational troubleshooting.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (sensor data transmission, operator command transmission, network adaptation, and latency visibility). DP-A is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. However, to ensure complete coverage and manage risk, the next level must decompose each sub-FR into detailed design parameters and implementation modules. High safety criticality demands rigorous decomposition and verification planning.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire sensor data (position, orientation, environmental) from the vehicle and transmit to the remote operator interface with latency ≤ 75 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall receive operator commands from the remote interface and transmit to the vehicle control subsystem with latency ≤ 75 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall implement network link monitoring and adaptive transmission strategies to maintain latency compliance under variable network conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall provide latency visibility and alerts to the operator and safety manager when latency approaches or exceeds safe thresholds.

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports hardware-accelerated packet processing and QoS prioritization.
Vehicle sensor subsystem provides deterministic, synchronized data streams at required sampling rates.
Operator interface hardware (display, input) supports sub-50 ms input-to-output latency.
Agreed network conditions are well-defined and representative of operational scenarios.
Teleoperation module has sufficient computational resources to implement protocol and aggregation logic.
Safety Services module can receive and act on latency alerts in real-time.
open_risks: Network infrastructure may not support required QoS; mitigation requires multi-path redundancy (DP-D).
Sensor jitter or asynchronous data arrival could violate latency bounds; mitigation requires sensor synchronization.
Operator interface latency could dominate end-to-end budget; mitigation requires careful hardware selection.
Fallback strategy (autonomous mode, reduced command frequency) may not be acceptable to operators; requires stakeholder validation.
Latency monitoring overhead could itself introduce latency; mitigation requires efficient instrumentation design.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure specification and QoS capabilities assessment.
Vehicle sensor subsystem timing analysis (sampling rate, jitter, synchronization).
Operator interface hardware latency measurement (display, input devices).
Protocol specification and implementation plan (language, libraries, testing strategy).
Latency budget allocation and trade-off analysis across all components.
Operator acceptance testing results under representative network conditions.
Safety analysis (FMEA, HARA) for latency-related failure modes.
Fallback strategy design and validation (autonomous mode, safety alerts).
